claim: Four-layer validation for AI agents in finance: pilot users for viability, red teaming for guardrail gaps, legal counsel for regulatory alignment, and automated compliance review
actionable: When deploying AI agents in regulated domains (finance, healthcare), use multi-layer validation: commercial pilots, adversarial testing, legal review, and automated compliance monitoring

claim: Input moderation and output moderation must run in parallel with different outcome groups (hard-block vs soft-block) for efficiency
actionable: Implement moderation as separate parallel tasks: PI detection runs independently without blocking, hard-blocks (toxic, jailbreak) block immediately, soft-blocks guide users to safer alternatives

claim: Orchestrator agents should route queries dynamically: API for real-time data, RAG for factual grounding, LLM for reasoning—not one-size-fits-all
actionable: Design agent orchestrators to analyze query intent and route to the safest execution method: prefer API calls for sensitive regulatory topics to avoid LLM hallucination

claim: Domain-driven bot architecture: each bot independently developed and deployed allows rapid iteration without impacting other components
actionable: Build AI agents as independent, specialized bots (one for customer service, one for compliance, one for research) that can be developed, tested, and deployed in parallel

claim: Model risk management is essential in finance; JP Morgan's 2012 trading loss ($2B) illustrates how models amplify risk when they underestimate volatility
actionable: In high-stakes domains (finance, healthcare), treat AI models as formal risk objects: establish governance, regularly audit for bias and calibration, and have human override mechanisms

claim: RAG systems require ground truth automation, memory-driven retrieval, and hyperparameter optimization to scale systematically
actionable: Build RAG libraries with automated ground-truth generation, memory integration, and hyperparameter tuning (top-K, chunk size, embedding model) for consistent performance

claim: Observability for agentic RAG systems is a major challenge; instrumentation at every stage (embedding, retrieval, moderation, LLM) is critical for debugging
actionable: Implement comprehensive observability: log embedding generation time, vector search latency, moderation speed, LLM token usage, and user feedback to diagnose agentic RAG failures

claim: Content ingestion for RAG requires delta-based processing, moderation guardrails on input, and automated data transformation for consistency
actionable: Design RAG content pipelines with delta-based incremental updates, apply the same guardrails to ingestion as to retrieval, and use automated chunking strategy tuning
